---
title: "Querying Google's Open Knowledge Format from R"
description: >
  Google's new OKF turns organizational knowledge into folders of markdown.
  Here's how to validate one, load it into a DuckDB catalog, and run semantic
  search over it — entirely from R.
date: "2026-06-22"
categories: [R, OKF, DuckDB, RAG, knowledge]
---

In June 2026, Google Cloud published the
[Open Knowledge Format (OKF)](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog) —
a vendor-neutral way to represent the curated knowledge that AI agents need as context.
The format is refreshingly boring: **a directory of markdown files with YAML
frontmatter**, one "concept" per file, with markdown links forming a graph. If you can
`cat` a file, you can read OKF.

That minimalism is the point — but it leaves a gap. Google shipped a *producer* (an
agent that turns BigQuery tables into OKF) and an HTML *visualizer*, and the community
quickly added validators. What was missing was a way to **load an OKF bundle into
something you can actually query** — and nothing existed in R at all.

So I wrote [**okf-ingest**](https://github.com/travisjakel/okf-ingest), an R + Python
package that reads a bundle, validates it, loads it into a portable **DuckDB** catalog,
and lets you **semantically search** it. This post walks the R side end to end.
(Disclosure: it's my package.)

## What an OKF bundle looks like

A concept file is just frontmatter + markdown:

```markdown
---
type: Metric
title: "Revenue"
description: "Total order value over a period."
tags: [finance, kpi]
timestamp: 2026-06-21T00:00:00Z
---

# Revenue

Computed from [orders](/orders.md).
```

The only field OKF *requires* is `type` — and it's a free string, not an enum, because
consumers must tolerate unknown types. Everything else (`title`, `description`, `tags`,
`timestamp`) is recommended. Links like `[orders](/orders.md)` are untyped edges that
turn the folder into a graph.

## Install

`okf` is on [R-universe](https://travisjakel.r-universe.dev):

```r
install.packages("okf", repos = c(
  travisjakel = "https://travisjakel.r-universe.dev",
  CRAN        = "https://cloud.r-project.org"
))
```

## Validate, permissively

OKF's conformance bar is deliberately tiny: every non-reserved `.md` file must have
parseable frontmatter with a non-empty `type`. Everything else — missing recommended
fields, broken links, unknown types — is a *finding*, never a rejection. `okf` follows
that to the letter:

```r
library(okf)

rd  <- okf_read("store")          # a bundle directory
val <- okf_validate(rd)
val
#>                 path severity        rule                              message
#> 1 metrics/revenue.md     warn broken_link unresolved link: /returns.md
```

One soft warning (a deliberately broken link in the example bundle), and the bundle is
still conformant. A real linter would `stop()`; OKF says load what you can and report
the rest.

## Load into a DuckDB catalog

`okf_ingest()` reads, validates, and writes everything into a DuckDB database:

```r
res <- okf_ingest("store", db_path = "kb.duckdb")
str(res$summary)
#> List of 8
#>  $ n_files     : int 5
#>  $ n_concepts  : int 3        # index.md / log.md are reserved, not concepts
#>  $ n_conformant: int 3
#>  $ conformant  : logi TRUE
#>  $ errors      : int 0
#>  $ warnings    : int 1
#>  $ links_total : int 8
#>  $ links_broken: int 1
```

From here it's *just DuckDB* — four tables (`okf_bundle`, `okf_concept`, `okf_link`,
`okf_validation`) you can hit with `DBI`:

```r
library(DBI)
con <- res$con

dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT path, type, title FROM okf_concept WHERE NOT reserved")
#>                 path           type    title
#> 1       customers.md BigQuery Table Customers
#> 2 metrics/revenue.md         Metric   Revenue
#> 3          orders.md BigQuery Table   Orders

# the concept graph
okf_graph_df(con)[, c("src_path", "dst_path", "resolved")]
```

Because the catalog is plain DuckDB, it's queryable from R, Python, or the bare `duckdb`
CLI — and it's a single file you can ship.

## The payoff: semantic search

This is where okf-ingest goes past the other tools. `okf_embed()` chunks each concept
body and embeds it (by default with a local [Ollama](https://ollama.com)
`nomic-embed-text` model — no data leaves your machine, no API key), storing the vectors
right in the catalog:

```r
okf_embed(con)            # -> number of chunks embedded
```

Then `okf_rag()` embeds a query and ranks chunks by cosine similarity, using DuckDB's
**native `list_cosine_similarity`** — no vector-database extension required:

```r
okf_rag(con, "how is revenue computed?", k = 3)[, c("path", "title", "score")]
#>                 path     title score
#> 1 metrics/revenue.md   Revenue 0.709
#> 2          orders.md    Orders 0.642
#> 3       customers.md Customers 0.478
```

The pluggable embedder is just a function of `texts` returning vectors, so swapping in
an OpenAI client (or anything else) is a one-liner.

## Ingest straight from GitHub

You don't need the bundle on disk. `okf_ingest()` accepts a git URL or a tar/zip
archive (local or remote); it clones/extracts to a temp dir, finds the bundle, and
cleans up after itself:

```r
res2 <- okf_ingest(
  "https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog.git",
  subdir  = "okf/samples/bitcoin",     # bundle within the repo
  db_path = "bitcoin.duckdb"
)
```

## Honest scope

OKF is two weeks old — "an invitation, not yet a standard." And the *validate/lint*
niche already has good tools (a Node validator, a pure-Rust crate). okf-ingest isn't
trying to be another linter. It's the only tool that turns a bundle into a
**SQL-queryable, semantically-searchable DuckDB catalog**, and the only one with R (and
Python) bindings — which is exactly the gap if you live in the tidy/DuckDB/arrow world.

If you're experimenting with agent context, knowledge bases, or just want a tidy way to
query a folder of markdown, give it a spin:

- **Repo:** <https://github.com/travisjakel/okf-ingest>
- **OKF spec:** <https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog>

Issues and PRs welcome.
